article_title: Fourth Power Nears Completion of Thermal Battery Prototype That Stores Grid Energy as White-Hot Heat in Liquid Tin

content_preview.title: Fourth Power Nears Completion of Thermal Battery Prototype That Stores Grid Energy as White-Hot Heat in Liquid Tin
content_preview.summary: The MIT spinout plans to demonstrate a 1 MWh thermal battery in Bedford, Massachusetts this year, using graphite bricks heated to 2,400 degrees Celsius and liquid tin to deliver grid-scale storage at a claimed $25 per kilowatt-hour, a fraction of lithium-ion costs.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Fourth Power, a startup spun out of MIT, is preparing to demonstrate a thermal energy storage system that heats graphite bricks to 2,400 degrees Celsius and pumps liquid tin through thermophotovoltaic cells to convert the stored heat back into electricity. The company plans to have a fully integrated 1-megawatt-hour demonstration unit operating at its Bedford, Massachusetts headquarters later this year, [according to MIT News](https://news.mit.edu/2026/turning-extreme-heat-large-sca...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-written science/energy news article explaining a novel thermal storage technology with clear technical descriptions and honest cost/efficiency comparisons.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 sources manually verified. MIT News confirmed 2400C graphite, liquid tin heat transfer, TPV cells >40% efficiency, 100 MW/acre power density, 1% daily heat loss, Asegun Henry founder, Guinness World Record ceramic pump, Bedford MA demonstration, 1 MWh prototype. TechCrunch confirmed $19M Series A from Breakthrough Energy Ventures and BVCC, $20M subsequent round Sept 2025. Interesting Engineering confirmed thermal battery concept and cost advantage vs lithium-ion.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All key claims verified: 2400C operating temperature, graphite brick dimensions (6 feet long, 20 inches thick), liquid tin at 232C melting point, >40% TPV efficiency, ~50% round-trip target, 100 MW/acre, $39M total funding, 25 MW/250 MWh full-scale unit, half football field footprint. The $25/kWh cost target is from the company and MIT News but was not confirmed by Interesting Engineering (which described it as 'far lower cost than lithium-ion' without specific figures). The $330/kWh lithium-ion comparison comes from MIT News context.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Balanced. Honestly notes 50% round-trip efficiency disadvantage versus lithium-ion and positions the technology within the broader long-duration storage landscape.
editor_notes.originality: Good contextualization within the long-duration storage landscape, referencing Form Energy iron-air and Noon Energy alternatives.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Well-verified energy technology news with appropriate caveats about unproven economics at scale. Approved.
